c-argument.content:
### The Same Sign, Different Meanings

The study examined eight icons used in global consumer software across twelve markets. The findings challenge the assumption that visual iconography is universally legible.

**The folder icon** — universally understood in markets where physical filing cabinets were common in living memory. Significantly less intuitive to users under 25 in markets where digital-first storage was the norm.

**The floppy disk for 'save'** — now a pure symbol (arbitrary) rather than an icon (resemblant). Users who have never seen a floppy disk still learn it — but they are learning a symbol, not recognising an object.

**The hamburger menu** — successful because it became conventional through saturation, not because it is inherently descriptive. Users who encounter it first in a financial app report higher confusion than those who encounter it first in a social app.

The conclusion: iconography is not a shortcut to universal legibility. It is a different kind of learning — one that can be faster than text in familiar contexts and slower in unfamiliar ones.

c-grid.alt: 8×12 grid showing icon recognition rates per market — darker cells indicate lower recognition

c-caption.text: Heat map of icon recognition rates across 12 markets. Darker cells indicate lower recognition. Note the age-correlated pattern on storage-related icons in digitally-saturated markets.
